key_paper_title: Evidence for polygenic adaptation to pathogens in the human genome
one_line_what_it_does: Gene-set enrichment test that aggregates per-gene selection scores (e.g. dN/dS, FST outlier rank) onto curated pathways/gene sets to detect polygenic/pathway-level signals of positive selection, correcting for gene-set size and overlap.
input_data_required: per-gene selection statistic (any single-gene score: dN/dS, FST, XP-CLR, etc.) + gene-set/pathway annotation (e.g. KEGG/Reactome/GO or a custom curated network partitioned into modules)
notes: Directly applicable: our per-gene RELAX K and aBSREL omega/p are exactly the kind of per-gene score PolySel consumes; our 168-node melanogenesis network or its node_class/module labels can serve as the gene sets.

method_or_tool: PolySel tool / primate ancestral-lineage pathway selection
key_paper_title: Detection of Pathways Affected by Positive Selection in Primate Lineages Ancestral to Humans
one_line_what_it_does: Applies the PolySel gene-set/pathway selection-enrichment framework specifically along ancestral primate branches, ranking pathways by combined per-gene branch-specific dN/dS signal.
input_data_required: per-gene branch-specific selection statistic (dN/dS or similar) across a primate phylogeny + pathway/gene-set definitions
notes: Closest published precedent to our proposed Tier-1/2 analysis: branch-specific selection (we have aBSREL per-branch omega) aggregated onto a primate tree by pathway/module.

method_or_tool: Network-based kernel-machine pathway test for GWAS risk pathways
key_paper_title: A network-based kernel machine test for the identification of risk pathways in genome-wide association studies
one_line_what_it_does: Kernel-machine (SKAT-type) test that incorporates gene-gene network topology as a similarity kernel to test whether a pathway/network module is jointly associated with a GWAS phenotype.
input_data_required: individual-level or summary GWAS genotype/phenotype data + a gene-gene interaction network structure to build the kernel
notes: Requires GWAS-scale genotype-phenotype association data (not selection statistics on a fixed species panel); not transferable to our cross-species selection-signal setting without substantial reformulation.

method_or_tool: HotNet2 heat-diffusion subnetwork discovery (module-level mutation burden)
key_paper_title: Pan-cancer network analysis identifies combinations of rare somatic mutations across pathways and protein complexes
one_line_what_it_does: Diffuses a per-gene mutation-burden score across a protein-protein interaction network via heat-kernel propagation, then extracts significantly heated connected subnetworks (modules) via permutation testing.
input_data_required: per-gene score (originally somatic mutation frequency, generalizable to any per-node score) + a network adjacency/interaction matrix
notes: Directly adaptable: substitute per-gene RELAX K (or aBSREL branch omega) as the diffused score on our signed/directed 168-node network; loses edge sign/direction information during diffusion, so this is a topology-agnostic-to-sign smoothing method (see Family 5).

key_paper_title: Similarity of phylogenetic trees as indicator of protein-protein interaction
one_line_what_it_does: Tests whether two proteins physically interact/co-evolve by correlating their pairwise-distance matrices (branch-length-derived) across a shared set of species; high correlation implies coupled evolutionary constraint.
input_data_required: multiple sequence alignments (or corresponding distance matrices) for each protein across the SAME set of species, ideally >15-20 species spanning meaningful divergence
notes: Directly runnable: our 117-species codon alignments for all 80 panel genes give per-gene distance matrices on a shared species set; mirrortree-style correlation of matrices for network-adjacent gene pairs (vs random non-adjacent pairs) tests whether regulator-effector pairs coevolve more than expected.

key_paper_title: Protein co-evolution, co-adaptation and interactions
one_line_what_it_does: Review of tree-similarity and residue-coevolution methods (mirrortree family, in-silico two-hybrid) for detecting/predicting protein-protein interaction and coupled evolutionary constraint from sequence/phylogenetic covariation.
input_data_required: multi-species alignments per protein; no expression data needed
notes: Useful methods overview; confirms family of methods needs only sequence/tree data, matching our inventory.

key_paper_title: Evolutionary rate covariation reveals shared functionality and coexpression of genes
one_line_what_it_does: Computes branch-wise relative evolutionary rates for each gene across a phylogeny (correcting for overall lineage effects) and correlates these rate profiles between gene pairs; high positive correlation across many species implies shared function, physical interaction, or coordinated selective pressure, independent of coexpression data.
input_data_required: per-branch relative substitution rates for each gene across a shared species tree (from gene-tree branch lengths or per-branch dN/dS-like statistics), same species set across all genes
notes: Highly applicable: our per-branch aBSREL omega estimates ARE per-branch rate profiles for each of the ~80 genes on the same 117-taxon tree; an ERC-style pairwise rate-correlation matrix across the network's regulator-effector edges is directly computable without any expression data.

key_paper_title: RERconverge: an R package for associating evolutionary rates with convergent traits
one_line_what_it_does: Computes relative evolutionary rates (RER) per gene per branch from a set of gene trees against a background species tree, then tests for correlation between RER and a binary/continuous convergent phenotype across independent origins, or between the RER profiles of gene pairs (ERC-style).
input_data_required: gene trees (or alignments) for each gene across a common species set + a species tree + (for trait association) a discrete/continuous trait mapped to tips or independent origins
notes: Very well matched to our data: we already have 14 independent dichromatism origins mapped on a 224-tip tree plus per-gene alignments; RERconverge's permulation-based trait-association test (RER vs. convergent phenotype, correcting for phylogeny) is essentially built for exactly this design, and its pairwise-RER mode gives an ERC analogue for network-adjacent gene pairs.

key_paper_title: RERconverge Expansion: Using Relative Evolutionary Rates to Study Complex Categorical Trait Evolution
one_line_what_it_does: Extends RERconverge to handle categorical (non-binary) and complex multi-state convergent traits when testing rate-trait association across a phylogeny.
input_data_required: same as RERconverge, with a multi-state/categorical trait coding
notes: Relevant if dichromatism/pigmentation phenotype states are coded as >2 categories rather than binary; otherwise the base RERconverge method suffices.

key_paper_title: Inferring the historical patterns of biological evolution
one_line_what_it_does: Introduces likelihood-based statistical models (including the lambda scaling parameter) for testing how strongly a continuous trait's cross-species variation is structured by phylogeny, and for fitting evolutionary models (e.g. rate variation) to comparative trait data.
input_data_required: a continuous or discrete trait value per tip species + a dated/topologically resolved phylogeny
notes: Directly usable: treat per-gene selection intensity (RELAX K) or per-branch omega as the 'trait', regress against network position (degree, betweenness, regulator-vs-effector class) using our species tree; phylogenetic signal in the network-position/rate association must be modeled, not ignored.

key_paper_title: phytools 2.0: an updated R ecosystem for phylogenetic comparative methods (and other things)
one_line_what_it_does: R package implementing phylogenetic generalized least squares (PGLS), phylogenetic signal estimation, ancestral state reconstruction, and many other comparative-method tools operating on a species tree plus tip trait data.
input_data_required: species tree (Newick/NEXUS) + tip-level trait values (continuous or discrete)
notes: Software directly usable on our primate_species_tree.nex: PGLS of per-gene RELAX K or aBSREL-summarized branch omega against node_class (regulator/effector), in-degree, out-degree, or betweenness computed from gene_network_edges.csv.

key_paper_title: A variable-rate quantitative trait evolution model using penalized-likelihood
one_line_what_it_does: Fits models allowing the rate of continuous-trait evolution to vary across branches of a phylogeny, rather than assuming a single global rate, via penalized likelihood.
input_data_required: species tree + tip continuous-trait data
notes: Could model per-branch selection intensity itself as a rate-heterogeneous trait along the tree, complementing aBSREL's own branch-specific omega estimates.

key_paper_title: Evolutionary rate in the protein interaction network
one_line_what_it_does: First large-scale claim that a protein's number of physical interaction partners (degree/connectivity) in a protein-protein interaction network negatively correlates with its rate of sequence evolution.
input_data_required: a protein-protein interaction network (degree per node) + a per-gene evolutionary rate estimate (dN/dS or similar) across a species comparison
notes: Directly testable with our data: node_class/degree from gene_network_edges.csv (168 nodes, ~309 edges) vs. our per-gene RELAX K/aBSREL omega; this is the topology-vs-rate test the seam explicitly calls for, must be reported alongside the confound literature below.

method_or_tool: Confound/critique: determinants of protein evolutionary rate
key_paper_title: Determinants of the rate of protein sequence evolution
one_line_what_it_does: Comprehensive review establishing that expression level (and correlated properties such as translation efficiency, protein abundance, and pleiotropy) is the dominant, and often confounding, correlate of protein evolutionary rate; network connectivity effects are frequently reduced or eliminated once expression is controlled for.
input_data_required: review; no new data, synthesizes expression, connectivity, dN/dS literature
notes: CRITICAL CAVEAT for our Tier plan: because we lack cross-species expression data, we cannot control for the expression-level confound this review identifies as the dominant driver of rate variation; any centrality-vs-rate result from our network must be reported as uncontrolled-for-expression and interpreted cautiously; this directly parallels the network-evo-explore finding of no centrality effect once other covariates (LOEUF, GTEx breadth) were included.

key_paper_title: Lethality and centrality in protein networks
one_line_what_it_does: Shows that highly-connected (hub) proteins in the yeast protein interaction network are disproportionately essential (lethal when deleted), motivating the broader 'central genes are more constrained' hypothesis later extended to evolutionary rate.
input_data_required: protein-interaction network topology + gene essentiality/knockout phenotype data
notes: We lack knockout/essentiality data for non-human primates; cited as conceptual precedent for the centrality-constraint hypothesis being tested via selection rate instead of essentiality.

key_paper_title: Comparative analysis of gene regulatory networks: from network reconstruction to evolution
one_line_what_it_does: Reviews computational approaches for reconstructing gene regulatory networks from multiple species and comparing them to infer edge gain/loss, rewiring events, and conserved/divergent regulatory architecture.
input_data_required: per-species regulatory network reconstructions, typically derived from ChIP-seq/ATAC-seq/expression data across each species, or comparative TF-binding-site predictions
notes: Ruled out as a direct method: edge-level rewiring inference (which regulatory link is gained/lost per species) requires per-species regulatory/expression evidence we do not have; our single curated human-centric network cannot itself be 'rewired' across the 117-primate panel without new data.

key_paper_title: Comparative genomics of gene regulation-conservation and divergence of cis-regulatory information
one_line_what_it_does: Reviews methods for comparing cis-regulatory sequences (enhancers, transcription-factor binding sites) across species to assess conservation and turnover of regulatory information.
input_data_required: cis-regulatory sequence alignments/annotations (enhancers, TFBS) across species, often paired with expression or chromatin data
notes: Ruled out for the current tiers: we have coding-sequence codon alignments, not regulatory-region alignments or chromatin/TFBS annotation; regulatory turnover analysis is out of scope without new noncoding data.

method_or_tool: Lineage-specific cis-regulatory module detection (OU models on TF binding)
key_paper_title: Identification of Lineage-Specific Cis-Regulatory Modules Associated with Variation in Transcription Factor Binding and Chromatin Activity Using Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Models
one_line_what_it_does: Applies Ornstein-Uhlenbeck phylogenetic models to chromatin/TF-binding intensity data across species to detect lineage-specific shifts in cis-regulatory activity.
input_data_required: quantitative TF-binding/chromatin signal per species (ChIP-seq-derived) + species phylogeny
notes: Requires chromatin/TF-binding assay data across species — not in our inventory; illustrates the OU-model class of method that would apply IF cross-species regulatory activity data existed.

key_paper_title: CoCoCoNet: conserved and comparative co-expression across a diverse set of species
one_line_what_it_does: Web resource/method for constructing and comparing gene coexpression networks across many species to identify conserved and divergent coexpression modules.
input_data_required: per-species RNA-seq expression compendia used to build coexpression networks
notes: Explicitly requires cross-species expression data, which is stated as absent from our inventory; this class of method (WGCNA/coexpression-network cross-species comparison) is ruled out as instructed.

method_or_tool: Network propagation for disease-gene / complex association
key_paper_title: Associating genes and protein complexes with disease via network propagation
one_line_what_it_does: Formalizes random-walk-with-restart network propagation: an initial per-node score (e.g. disease association) is iteratively smoothed across network edges so that connected/nearby nodes accumulate elevated combined scores, improving detection of weakly-scored but network-proximal genes.
input_data_required: a network (adjacency matrix, can be weighted/directed) + an initial per-node score vector
notes: Directly applicable as a smoothing operator: our directed signed 168-node network + per-gene RELAX K (or per-branch aBSREL omega, summarized per gene) as initial scores can be propagated to test whether selection signal clusters near strongly-selected 'seed' genes along the regulatory topology; edge sign is typically discarded by standard propagation (a caveat to flag), and directionality can optionally be preserved by using the directed adjacency matrix instead of its symmetrized version.

key_paper_title: Pan-cancer network analysis identifies combinations of rare somatic mutations across pathways and protein complexes
one_line_what_it_does: Heat-diffusion module-discovery method (see Family 1 entry for detail).
input_data_required: per-gene score + network adjacency
notes: Same tool as the Family 1 entry; listed again because it is simultaneously a propagation method (diffusion kernel) and a module-detection/pathway-selection tool; the two families are not mutually exclusive for this method.

key_paper_title: Hierarchical HotNet: identifying hierarchies of altered subnetworks
one_line_what_it_does: Extends HotNet-style heat-diffusion to extract a HIERARCHY of altered subnetworks at multiple size/strength thresholds simultaneously, rather than a single flat set of modules, improving robustness to the choice of a single significance cutoff.
input_data_required: per-gene score + network adjacency (same as HotNet2)
notes: Preferred over flat HotNet2 if selection-signal propagation is applied, because it avoids committing to one arbitrary module-size threshold; still discards edge sign during diffusion unless adapted.

key_paper_title: Network propagation: a universal amplifier of genetic associations
one_line_what_it_does: Reviews the mathematical basis, variants (random walk with restart, heat diffusion, insulated diffusion), and applications of network propagation for amplifying weak per-gene signals using network topology, across genetics/genomics use cases.
input_data_required: review; general requirements as for propagation methods above
notes: Best entry point for choosing/justifying a propagation variant for our directed signed network; discusses caveats (degree bias, need for permutation-based null) directly relevant to our small (~168-node) network size.

key_paper_title: RELAX: detecting relaxed selection in a phylogenetic framework
one_line_what_it_does: Codon model test comparing selection-intensity parameter K between a foreground and background branch set; K>1 = intensified selection, K<1 = relaxed selection.
input_data_required: codon alignment + phylogeny with foreground/background branch partition
notes: Already generated for our full 117-primate panel (relax_results.csv); provides the per-gene K scores that feed Families 1-3 and 5 above.

key_paper_title: Less is more: an adaptive branch-site random effects model for efficient detection of episodic diversifying selection
one_line_what_it_does: Fits a branch-site random-effects codon model per branch, testing each branch independently for episodic positive selection (omega, p-value), without requiring a priori foreground/background labeling.
input_data_required: codon alignment + phylogeny
notes: Already generated per-branch across the 117-taxon tree (absrel/*.ABSREL.json); provides the branch-resolved omega/p that ERC/RERconverge-style rate-covariation analyses and PGLS-on-network-position analyses would consume.
